item.title: The Mid-Year Reset: Your Career's Most Underrated Checkpoint
item.body:
The calendar just crossed the halfway line. Six months of 2026 are gone, six remain — and this stretch at the end of June is the most overlooked moment on the professional calendar. Everyone runs a review in December. Almost no one runs one in June, which is exactly why it works: you still have half a year to act on what you find.

Here's a 30-minute audit worth doing before the summer slips away.

## 1. Score your January self

Pull up the goals you set in the new year — the real ones, not the aspirational fluff. For each, mark it **on track**, **stalled**, or **quietly abandoned**. No judgment. The point is data, not guilt. Most people discover two or three goals they've silently dropped and one they've already outgrown.

## 2. Audit your energy, not just your output

Look back at the past six months and ask a sharper question than "what did I accomplish?" Ask: *which work made time disappear, and which drained me?* Careers rarely stall because of a lack of skill. They stall because people spend years doing work that quietly exhausts them. Name the energizing tasks — that's your compass for the next six months.

## 3. Refresh the record while it's fresh

Update three things now, not in a panic later:

- **Your resume** — add the wins from the first half of the year while the details are still sharp.
- **Your professional profile** — a current headline and a recent accomplishment keep you visible to opportunity.
- **Your "brag document"** — a private running list of what you shipped, saved, or solved. It's your best friend at review time.

## 4. Pick one relationship to rebuild

Choose a single former colleague, mentor, or contact you've lost touch with and send a genuine, no-agenda message. Networks decay in silence and compound with attention. One reconnection every month adds up faster than any grand strategy.

## The half-year advantage

A December review is a post-mortem — the year is already spent. A June review is a course correction, with real road left to drive. Spend half an hour now, and October's you will be grateful.
